coredata.dc:title: Dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of silver ions contamination in real sample
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hengye
Wang, Shuo
Fu, Haiyan
Xie, Hongliang
Lan, Wei
Xu, Lu
Zhang, Lei
She, Yuanbin
coredata.dc:description: Silver ions, as a commonly used industrial heavy metal, tends to deposit in the body and induce many diseases. In this work, modified CdTe QDs with green and red emission were synthesized to assemble dual-QDs, which could be efficient and selective utilized for Ag+ determination through the electron transfer progress between surface functional group of dual-QDs and Ag+, and the aggregation of Ag+ on the surface of dual-QDs. Under the appropriate pH value and volume ratio, the interaction between the surface functional groups of assembled dual-QDs reduce the affinity of Hg2+ in this system. The fluorescent signal of dual-QDs simultaneously attenuation or enhancement in the same proportion remove the interference of Cu2+ and other metal ions. Therefore, this method can selectively detect Ag+ without any masking agents. The linear region of detection was from 0 to 800 nmol/L (R2 > 0.998), and low of detection (LOD) was 7.7 nmol/L, which could meet the corresponding standards of World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). This effective proposed dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe has been applied to detect Ag+ in real environment water, tea and Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium (CRP) water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sliver ions determination
Ratios fluorescent probe
Electron transfer progress
Surface aggregation effect

Ratios fluorescence response of dual-QDs to Ag+
The possible mechanism of the ratios fluorescent probe to Ag+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The selectivity of green QDs (A), red QDs (B) and dual-QDs (C, inset is the corresponding visualized results) exposure to different kinds of metal ions. The fluorescence spectra of the reaction between dual-QDs and different metal ions (D). The concentration of Ag+ is 0.5 μmol/L, and the concentration of other metal ions is 5 μmol/L. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra of dual-QDs in the presence of Ag+ at various concentrations (0–800 nmol/L) under optimal conditions. Inset is linear fitter diagram of Ired/Igreen versus concentrations of Ag+.
Schematic of the dual-QDs sensing of Ag+.
Comparison of this work with various reported methods.
The fitting date of fluorescence lifetime results.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: τ1 and τ2 were fast and slow fluorescence lifetime.
τavg: the average value of fluorescence lifetime.
A1 and A2 were the corresponding weight factor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The recovery results of Ag+ in real sample.

head.ce:title: Dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of silver ions contamination in real sample

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, Guizhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, Guizhou, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hengye Chen and Shuo Wang equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver ions, as a commonly used industrial heavy metal, tends to deposit in the body and induce many diseases. In this work, modified CdTe QDs with green and red emission were synthesized to assemble dual-QDs, which could be efficient and selective utilized for Ag+ determination through the electron transfer progress between surface functional group of dual-QDs and Ag+, and the aggregation of Ag+ on the surface of dual-QDs. Under the appropriate pH value and volume ratio, the interaction between the surface functional groups of assembled dual-QDs reduce the affinity of Hg2+ in this system. The fluorescent signal of dual-QDs simultaneously attenuation or enhancement in the same proportion remove the interference of Cu2+ and other metal ions. Therefore, this method can selectively detect Ag+ without any masking agents. The linear region of detection was from 0 to 800 nmol/L (R2 > 0.998), and low of detection (LOD) was 7.7 nmol/L, which could meet the corresponding standards of World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). This effective proposed dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe has been applied to detect Ag+ in real environment water, tea and Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium (CRP) water.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of economic, science and technology, people's living standard has been greatly improved, however, it has also led to a series of environmental pollution problems, especially heavy metal pollution [1]. The release of heavy metals into the environment, even at low concentrations, will have serious harm on the surrounding environment and organisms [2]. According to the standards of the World Health Organization (WHO), presence of Ag+ less than 0.05 ppm (0.05 g/mL) is safe for human body, any concentration above this could lead to various adverse health effects [3]. As is known to all, Ag+, a rare but highly demanded and cost-effective metal catalyst, which is widely used in jewelry, pharmaceuticals, biomedicine, electronic equipment, photography, imaging and other industries [5–9]. However, researchers have shown that Ag+ can damage the activity of enzymes and combine with various metabolites such as amine, imidazole and carboxyl, causing serious consequences to environmental safety and human health [10–12]. Therefore, to design and develop a simple, quick, sensitive and selective method to detect Ag+ is of great significance for the environmental safety and human health.

Conventional methods including inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry, voltammetry, flame atomic absorption, ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) and electrochemical method, are extensively used to detect Ag+ [13–17]. However, these methods have various shortcomings which often require complicated operation procedures, large sample volumes, sophisticated instrumentation, time-consuming analysis, specialized skills and interfered from closely related metal ions [18,19]. Spectroscopic method, especially fluorescence, has attracted great attentions because of its high sensitivity, selectivity, simplicity and does not use any expensive instrument or complicated operations and detection procedures [20]. In addition, it has been found that the detection of heavy metal ions by fluorescence method can well solve the shortcomings of other detection methods [21].

With the rapid development of nanometer science and technology, fluorescent nanomaterials have unique advantages in the design of various fluorescent sensors [22]. Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanomaterials which received considerable attention because of its unique properties, such as narrow and symmetry emission spectrum, wide excitation spectrum, relatively high quantum yield, large stokes shift effectively, good selectivity, high sensitivity and superior photochemical stability [23–25]. Particularly, some efforts have devoted to the analysis of Ag+ by using single QDs as the fluorescent probe based on their quenching (turn-off) or enhancement (turn-on) of the fluorescence intensities [26,27]. However, these single-intensity-based sensors usually lack self-calibration and suffer background interference and the effect of visualization analysis is monotonous. Besides, such approaches are probably unsuitable for one-step quick detection due to the sophisticated and time consuming analytical procedures, and most of them are susceptible to severe interference from other metal ions and typically require masking agents, which are costly and often highly harmful to the environment and human body [28,29,40]. As is known to all that the ratios fluorescence method based on dual-emission or multi-emission can overcome the above mentioned disadvantages effectively, so that it can allow for the detection of analytes in the complex samples through the signal of ratios fluorescence at different wavelength [30,31]. What's more, the ratios fluorescent probes can also improve sensitivity and selectivity by eliminating the interference of background and metal ions in complex samples with the built-in correction and realize visualization analysis based on more sensitive and intuitive polychromatic variation [32,33]. Therefore, the ratio fluorescence method may be a simple, sensitive and intuitive method for detection of Ag+ [34].

For the present work, NAC and TGA modified CdTe with different particle sizes and assembled dual-QDs were synthesized for the determination of Ag+ based on ratios fluorescent method. It was found that this method has a wide linear range of 0–800 nmol/L and a low detection limit of 7.7 nmol/L which meet the standards of WHO and EPA and could be utilized as an ultrasensitive and selective fluorescent sensor for determination of trace concentrations of Ag+. Moreover, there is no interference even in the presence of ten times concentration of other metal ions, no need for poisonous masking agents, with high selectivity, thus realize the maximum reduction of environmental pollution. Furthermore, the method can be applied to the measurement of Ag+ in real samples with satisfactory results. In this paper, a simple, rapid, time-saving, low-cost and sensitive Ag+ detection method based on ratios fluorescence of dual-QDs with different excitation wavelengths, has been established for the first time, which has a broad application prospect.

Analytical grade of Cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O), Thioglycolic acid (TGA), Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), Sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), a variety of metal salts (Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+ et al.) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China). N-acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Water soluble TGA-capped CdTe (green QDs) and NAC-capped CdTe (red QDs) were synthesized in our laboratory. Deionized distilled water (DDW) was prepared from Molgeneral 220a water purification system (Molecular). Tea and CRP (Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium) were provided from National Resource Center for Chinese Materia Medica, China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences (Beijing, China).

The fluorescence spectrum of this work was measured by using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and the excitation wavelength (λex) of the whole work was set to 360 nm. Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700-FT-IR spectrometer (USA) and UV-1800PC spectrophotometer (Mapada, China) were used to record the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and zeta (ζ) potential results were performed by using TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN (USA) and Zetasizer Nano ZS 90 (Tai Pei, China), respectively. Fluorescent lifetime (FLT) results were acquired by using Edinburgh FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Britain).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The green-emission CdTe QDs were synthesized by partial modification from literature [35,36]. Briefly, 18 μL TGA was added into 0.0457 g CdCl2·2.5H2O dissolved in 50 mL DDW under stirring. After dissolving completely, the pH was adjusted to 10.3 by dropwise titrating NaOH solution (1.0 mol/L). Then 0.0089 g Na2TeO3 was added into above-mentioned solution under the protection of N2. When the reaction went through 5 min, 80 mg NaBH4 was injected into above container of solution. Finally, resulting solution was filled the N2 for another 5 min and refluxed at 100 °C for 1.5 h under open-air conditions.

The red-emission CdTe QDs synthesis step was similar to the above process. In brief, 0.1142 g CdCl2·2.5H2O and 0.0979 g NAC was dissolved in 40 mL DDW under stirring. The pH of Cd (II)-NAC solution was adjusted to 10.7. Then 0.0218 g Na2TeO3 and 0.012 g NaBH4 was added into Cd (II)-NAC solution under N2. In the end, the above solution was refluxed at 100 °C for 1 h under open-air conditions.

In order to get pure CdTe QDs solution, green and red CdTe QDs was filtered by 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove impurities. Further, the crude CdTe QDs solution was centrifugated and washed by ethanol. Then, green and red QDs were dispersed in DDW with specific volume ratios to prepare dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probes. At last, the prepared solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further application. With rhodamine B and rhodamine 6G as reference and combined with Eq. (1), QYs of green and red quantum dots were calculated as 51.60% and 22.93%, respectively.
(1)
Φ =
Φ R
I S
/
I R
×
A R
/
A S
×
η S
/
η R
2
where “Φ” is the quantum yield, “I” is the measured integrated emission intensity, “A” represents the value of absorbance, “η” is the refractive index of the solution. The subscript “R” refers to reference substance and “S” for QDs sample.

In our experiments, all fluorescence measurements were performed at room temperature and pressure. In a typical analysis, 100 μL dual-QDs was diluted by 800 μL NaOH solution, followed by adding 100 μL Ag+ at different concentration to keep the total volume at 1 mL. After 5 min, the fluorescence intensity of mixed solution was acquired following excitation at 360 nm. Besides, the instrument parameters of voltage and dual-slit widths (λex, λem) were set to 400 V and 10 nm.
Analysis of Ag+ in real samples

The south Lake water and tap water was filtered through nylon membrane filter (0.22 μm). The tea and CRP were made with boiling water for 15 min, following filtered to obtain tea and CRP water. All real samples do not require further processing. Afterwards, real samples were used as solvents to prepare Ag+ solutions of different concentrations. The following reagent addition steps and the instrument parameters were the same as in Section 2.4.

The TEM images of synthesized green CdTe QDs, red CdTe QDs and dual-QDs were displayed respectively in Fig. S1. The result showed that the size and dispersion of the QDs were uniform, and the average particle size of the green CdTe QDs, red CdTe QDs and dual CdTe QDs were 2.72 nm, 3.23 nm and 3.24 nm (Fig. S1A, B, C), which was in agreement with the result of the empirical fitting formula [37]:
(2)
D =
9.8127 ×
10
− 7
λ 3
−
1.7147 ×
10
− 3
λ 2
+ 1.0064 λ − 194.84

In the above formula, D (nm) is the size of CdTe QDs, and λ (nm) is the wavelength of the first excitonic absorption peak of the corresponding CdTe QDs. According to the formula, the average particle size of the green QDs and the red QDs were 2.79 nm and 3.32 nm, respectively.

The FT-IR analysis was used to investigate the surface functional groups of QDs, as shown in Fig. S2A. The characteristic absorption band of SH at 2600–2500 cm−1 was disappeared, which indicated the successful synthesis of green and red QDs [35]. The broad band centered at about 3430 cm−1 was derived from the stretching vibration of OH. The band at 1420 cm−1 and 1010 cm−1 were belonged to bending vibrations of –CH3 and CH. Under the same condition, the peak attenuation of dual-QDs indicated that the corresponding number of functional groups decreased, the enhancement of molecular symmetry and the decrease of dipole moment, which indicated that dual-QDs was assembled by interaction of functional groups on the surface of red and green QDs.

For obtaining the optimal Ag+ detection condition, the affecting factors included volume ratios, pH and reaction time of dual-QDs were studied. It can be seen from Fig. S3 that green QDs (1.31 × 10−6 mol/L) and red QDs (3.94 × 10−6 mol/L) with different volume ratios (green: red) directly affected the response of fluorescent probes to Hg2+ and Ag+. When the value of the volume ratios were decreased, the sensitivity of dual-QDs to Ag+ gradually increased and tended to be stable within a certain range, meanwhile, the sensitivity of dual-QDs towards Hg2+ was weaken. Consequently, the addition of the red QDs can not only improve the response of the ratios fluorescent probe to Ag+, but also mask the interference of Hg2+ to achieve the enhancement of the selectivity for Ag+. In general, the volume ratio of 1:1 was selected as the optimal condition for this experiment. The effect of pH had also been studied in this work. Sodium hydroxide solutions with different pH values were selected for screening. As shown in Fig. S4, in different pH conditions, ratios fluorescent signal had slight influence to the response of Ag+, but the response to Hg2+ was large. In our previous work, green CdTe was undoubtedly unstable under over acid conditions [49], because the modified S− and external –COO– were willing to combine with H+ to form relatively stable -SH and –COOH, which caused the QDs to lose activity. Meanwhile, the pH conditions that too alkali can also reduce the stability of the combining of red and green QDs, making dual-QDs easier to combine with Hg2+. Because the formation of mercury oxide (HgO) was induced under strong base conditions, and HgO may have a great response to the dual-QDs. Appropriate pH level was of significance to the activity and stability of QDs, which was the reason that the pH was generally adjusted to 8–11 in the synthesis process of QDs [50,51]. Hence, pH 11 was selected as the optimal condition for our work. At the same time, as shown in Fig. S5, the optimal response time was 5 min.

The selectivity of green, red QDs and dual-QDs on various common metal ions (including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Sn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+) were investigated (Fig. 1 ), It's clearly that green QDs has a good response to Ag+, Cu2+ and Hg2+ (Fig. 1A), and red QDs has a good response to Cu2+ (Fig. 1B). However, dual-QDs have high ratios fluorescent signal response to Ag+ (Fig. 1C, D), merely. In the fluorescent visualized results, under UV light, dual-QDs reacted with Ag+ and then produced a distinct red color that no other metal ion can achieve (Fig. 1C). Besides, since both green and red QDs have a better response to Cu2+, the signal response of Cu2+was eliminated by ratios fluorescence. In Fig. S6, in a fairly wide concentration range of Cu2+, the ratios fluorescent signal basically unchanged after the reaction of dual-QDs and Cu2+, which hardly affected the detection of Ag+ in this system. Similarly, other metal ions that might have a slight response to green and red QDs were also removed through ratios fluorescent signal. The UV spectra about the selective of dual-QDs to different metal ions was also studied. From the results of Fig. S7, we can observe that dual-QDs reacted with Ag+, merely, and the green QDs produced a red-shift about 15 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratios fluorescence response of dual-QDs to Ag+
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence emission spectra of dual-QDs in the presence of different concentrations of Ag+ were presented in Fig. 2 . With the increasing concentrations of Ag+ from 0 to 800 nmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of green QDs decreased gradually, and produced obvious red-shift from 540 nm to 570 nm. This indicated that Ag+ may bind to the surface of quantum dots, which was similar to the results reported in the literature [26,39]. When the concentration of Ag+ reached 400 nmol/L, some red QDs started to interact with the Ag+ and correspondingly released some green QDs from dual-QDs. At this concentration, the response of the released green QDs to the Ag+ probably were stronger than green QDs of the dual-QDs, resulting in two fitted linear equations in our linear range. In our system, the ratios fluorescence (Ired/Igreen) showed great linearity with the concentration of Ag+ from 0 to 800 nmol/L with a detection limit of 7.7 nmol/L (Fig. 2) (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ and k were the standard deviation of blank values and the slope of linear equation, respectively). The fitted linear equations can be expressed as:
(3)
y = 0.0012 4x + 0.40126
R 2
= 0.998
(4)
y = 0.0042 0x − 0.78484
R 2
= 0.998
where x represented the concentration of Ag+, and y was the value of Ired/Igreen. When the concentration of Ag+ was 0–400 nmol/L and 400–800 nmol/L, it was applicable to Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively. The linear range of our method was wide enough and the LOD was lower than the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) drinking water standard for Ag+ (about 900 nmol/L) [26]. Therefore, it was feasible to detect silver pollution with ratios fluorescence of dual-QDs. Table 1 compared different fluorescence methods for Ag+ detection in other references. Obviously, the range of linearity in our system was pretty wide, and the limit of detection was the same level as methods recently reported [4,35,40]. Besides, one of the significant advantages of our method was that the addition of masking agent was not required and without disturbing by other metal ions, such as Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ and Zn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism of the ratios fluorescent probe to Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Several mechanisms have been confirmed to explain the “turn-off” or “turn-on” phenomenon of reaction between QDs and metal ions. Through XPS, FT-IR, zeta potential and other experiments, Chen et al. [40] found that QDs were quenched by Ag+ via an electron transfer process, which was the most common quenching method. Besides, other researchers also found that cation exchange [41], inner filter effect [42], energy transfer [43] and surface adsorption [44] or aggregation [45] can be interpreted as the mechanism of quantum dots quenching by metal ions.

In our system, fluorescence lifetime, FT-IR, zeta potential, TEM and UV spectra were used to explain the sensing mechanism of the ratios fluorescent probe to Ag+, respectively. According to the Eq. (5), the average fluorescence lifetime value can be calculated.
(5)
τ avg
=
A 1
×
τ 1
2
+
A 2
×
τ 2
2
/
A 1
×
τ 1
+
A 2
×
τ 2

Based on the date described in Table 2 , we can easily calculate the fluorescence lifetime of green QDs before and after the reaction with Ag+ were 27.5 ns and 20.2 ns, respectively. However, at the same concentration of Ag+, the fluorescence of green QDs was almost quenched by Ag+ completely. The value of τ0/τ was close to 1, which was far less than the value of F0/F. Therefore, the main quenching was concluded to be static in nature.

Where τ1 and τ2 were fast and slow fluorescence lifetime, A1 and A2 were the weighting factors for τ1 and τ2, respectively. τ0/τ and F0/F were the ratios of average fluorescence life and fluorescence intensity before and after the reaction, respectively.

Fig. S2B showed the FT-IR results of dual-QDs interacting with Ag+ and Hg+ respectively. The various forms of stretching vibration and bending vibration of dual-QDs were basically unchanged after the addition of Hg2+. However, after adding Ag+, the FT-IR spectrum of dual-QDs changed greatly and the shape of peak was tended to be the single QDs, among which the most obvious was that the stretching vibration of OH decreased from 3430 cm−1 to 3360 cm−1. In the presence of Ag+, electrons of dual-QDs surface functional group tended to move towards Ag+, and the density of the OH electron cloud decreased, thus reducing the frequency and corresponding wavenumber of OH stretching vibration. The FT-IR results showed that the interaction between Ag+ and surface functional group of dual-QDs was via electron transfer [40,48]. Besides, the red-shift of the fluorescence spectrum of green QDs in dual-QDs can also be illustrated an electron transfer progress between dual-QDs and Ag+ [35], which was mainly attributed to the fact that the Ksp value of Ag2S is much less than that of CdS, and caused the electrons transfer from S2− to Ag+ to form more stable AgS compounds.

Moreover, zeta potential of dual-QDs also changed in presence of Ag+ (Fig. S8). When Ag+ added into the dual-QDs solution, zeta potential of the mixed solution increased from −24.0 mV to +16.9 mV. But after adding Hg2+, the zeta potential added from −24.0 mV to −20.5 mV, merely. It's clearly indicated that dual-QDs surface functional groups were negatively charged, Ag+ was tended to coalesce with dual-QDs surface functional groups and aggregated on the particle surface to make the particle positively charged. In other words, the aggregation of Ag+ on the surface of dual-QDs lowered the absolute value of zeta potential. This result represented that the particles of QDs in the solution became unstable and non-dispersed or probably made sliver nanoparticles (AgNPs), which was consistent with TEM characterization of Fig. S1C, E. In addition, the addition of Ag+ caused a significant red-shift in the UV spectra of dual-QDs. This situation may be due to the binding of Ag and Te and the subsequent formation of AgTe structure on dual-QDs surface traps [46,47], which effectively eliminated the dangling bonds of Te on the particle surface [40].

In general, as shown in Fig. 3 , the above experimental results showed that the possible mechanism of the ratios fluorescent probe monitoring Ag+ could be summed up in following two points: 1) fluorescence quenching caused by electron transfer progress between surface functional group of dual-QDs and Ag+; 2) accumulation and aggregation of Ag+ on the surface of dual-QDs particles reduce the stability and inherent activity of dual-QDs.

To validate the feasibility and accuracy of the proposed method in the practical applications, four solvent environments were considered as real samples for the detection of Ag+. In this work, we tested south lake water and tap water, which were likely to be contaminated by Ag+ in industrial production. In addition, tea and CRP have been very popular in China since ancient times. Making tea and CRP with silver utensils was popular in China especially among the minorities, but the phenomenon of inferior silver was rampant, so we considered tea water and CRP water. All the tests were performed 3 times and the results were listed in Table 3 . From the results, recovery of Ag+ in different real water samples obtained was in a range of 95.5–107.3% and relative standard deviations (RSD) range from 0.2 to 2.6% (n = 3). It was shown that the response of the ratios fluorescent testing platform was not affected by complicate interference matrices from environmental water and daily water samples. The high recovery percentages and small standard deviations demonstrated that the proposed method hold great potential for the specific identification and quantification of Ag+ with high accuracy and practicability in different real complex water samples.

A rapid, low-cost, high selective and sensitive fluorescence method based on ratios fluorescent probe of dual-QDs without any masking agents or other additive was developed for the first time to determinate silver ions. Through optimizing the pH, reaction time and dual-QDs volume ratios to obtain the optimal reaction conditions. The results figured out that there were fluorescence quenching and linear spectral red-shift with concentration of Ag+ increasing from 0 to 800 nmol/L, and the LOD was received to be 7.7 nmol/L, which was meet WHO and EPA standard. In addition, the interaction between the surface functional groups of green and red QDs in assembled dual-QDs makes it possible to detect Ag+ directly, efficiently and sensitively without the addition of masking agents or other chemical reagents. Moreover, the possible mechanism can be attributed to the effect of electron transfer and the aggregation of Ag+ on dual-QDs surface. Finally, tap water, south lake water, CRPs water and tea water were used as the real samples for our system and the results showed a pretty result of recovery experiment which indicated that the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe can be used to detect Ag+ contamination in our daily life.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Hengye Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Shuo Wang: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Haiyan Fu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Hongliang Xie: Writing - original draft. Wei Lan: Resources. Lu Xu: Formal analysis. Lei Zhang: Formal analysis. Yuanbin She: Supervision.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported from the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grants nos. 31972164, 21776321, 21706233], Key Projects of Technological Innovation of Hubei Province [grants no. 2016ACA138], the Open Research Program [grants nos. 2015ZD001, 2015ZD002 and 2015ZY006] from the Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei province (South-Central University for Nationalities), the Talented Youth Cultivation Program from “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities”, and South-Central University for National [grants no. CZP20007].
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118248.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Macrobenthos functional trait responses to heavy metal pollution gradients in a temperate lagoon
Environ. Pollut.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.Y. Hu, J.Y. Dong, L.J. Gao, X.L. Yang, Z. Wang, X.M. Zhang, Macrobenthos functional trait responses to heavy metal pollution gradients in a temperate lagoon, Environ. Pollut. 253 (2019) 1107-1116. doi:10.1016/j.envpol.2019.06.117.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Meta analysis of heavy metal pollution and sources in surface sediments of Lake Taihu, China
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Niu, X. Jiang, K. Wang, J.D. Xia, W. Jiao, Y. Niu, H. Yu, Meta analysis of heavy metal pollution and sources in surface sediments of Lake Taihu, China, Sci. Total Environ. 700 (2020) 134509. doi:10.1016/j.scitotenv.2019.134509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gallotannin functionalized gold nanoparticles for rapid, selective and sensitive colorimetric detection of Ag+ in aqueous medium: approach to eco-friendly water analysis
Sensors Actuators B Chem.
